Client 2: And I want a clean plan—like, what to do with the accounts and how tax-efficient we are.
Advisor: Perfect. Let’s start with the cash-flow basics, then we’ll map the accounts and any insurance. Jennifer, William—what do you have coming in in total each year, gross, roughly?
Client 2: Combined gross is 63600 a year.
Advisor: Got it—thank you. And on the spending side, what do your total monthly expenses usually run, all-in?
Client 1: We track it pretty closely—3050 a month.

Client 2: Combined gross is 63600 a year.
Advisor: Got it—thank you. And on the spending side, what do your total monthly expenses usually run, all-in?
Client 1: We track it pretty closely—3050 a month.
Advisor: Okay. And do you have any monthly debt payments—mortgage, car loans, credit cards—anything like that?
Client 2: No, zero. Monthly debt payments are 0.

Client 2: And I want a clean plan—like, what to do with the accounts and how tax-efficient we are.
Advisor: Perfect. Let’s start with the cash-flow basics, then we’ll map the accounts and any insurance. Jennifer, William—what do you have coming in in total each year, gross, roughly?
Client 2: Combined gross is 63600 a year.
Advisor: Got it—thank you. And on the spending side, what do your total monthly expenses usually run, all-in?
Client 1: We track it pretty closely—3050 a month.

Client 2: No, zero. Monthly debt payments are 0.
Advisor: Great. One sec—just noting that down. Now, shifting to what you’ve built up: about how much do you have in investable assets total, across retirement and taxable accounts?
Client 2: Total investable assets are 411000.
Advisor: Thanks. Let’s break that 411000 down so I understand what’s where. William, do you have a taxable account—like a brokerage or bank-held investment account—and what’s the value?
Client 2: Yes. I’ve got a taxable account held at a bank—value is 227850.

Client 2: Yeah, I’ve got a private markets piece on a retirement platform—16700.
Advisor: Okay—thank you. Now, I also want to capture the home, even though it’s not investable in the same way. Jennifer, do you consider your primary residence part of the picture here—what’s the current value you use?
Client 1: Yes—primary residence value is 275300.
Advisor: Got it. And just to confirm on liabilities: besides the fact you said no monthly debt payments, do you have any loans outstanding at all right now?
Client 2: No loans outstanding.

Client 2: And I want a clean plan—like, what to do with the accounts and how tax-efficient we are.

Advisor: Hi Jennifer, hi William—nice to meet you both. I’m glad you came in. Before we get into the weeds, what prompted you to set this up now?
Client 1: Thanks. For me it’s retirement timing and just… making sure we’re not missing anything big.
Client 2: And I want a clean plan—like, what to do with the accounts and how tax-efficient we are.
Advisor: Perfect. Let’s start with the cash-flow basics, then we’ll map the accounts and any insurance. Jennifer, William—what do you have coming in in total each year, gross, roughly?
Client 2: Combined gross is 63600 a year.

Advisor: Okay. Jennifer, on your side, do you have a workplace retirement plan—401(k) or IRA—and about what’s in it?
Client 1: I do—my 401(k)/IRA bucket is 127750, held at a bank.
Advisor: And any other accounts in your name—like a brokerage account or even just a separate bank or cash account you consider part of investments?
Client 1: There’s another one—held at a brokerage—and it’s 38700.
Advisor: Great. William, anything more specialized—private investments, private markets, that sort of thing?

Client 1: It’s listed as Other.
Advisor: Understood. And when you think about your marginal tax bracket, what band are you typically in right now?
Client 2: We’re in the 32% bracket.
Advisor: Thanks—let me jot that down.
Advisor: Moving on to income—what do you have as your gross income per year overall?

Advisor: Okay—thanks, Jennifer. Before we switch gears, just a quick household question: it’s the two of you living at home—so number of adults is 2?
Client 1: Yes, number of adults is 2.
Advisor: Thanks. And you two are married?
Client 2: Yes, we’re married.
Advisor: Great. For planning purposes, what state do you consider your residence for tax purposes?

Advisor: Hi Jennifer, hi William—nice to meet you both. I’m glad you came in. Before we get into the weeds, what prompted you to set this up now?
Client 1: Thanks. For me it’s retirement timing and just… making sure we’re not missing anything big.
Client 2: And I want a clean plan—like, what to do with the accounts and how tax-efficient we are.
Advisor: Perfect. Let’s start with the cash-flow basics, then we’ll map the accounts and any insurance. Jennifer, William—what do you have coming in in total each year, gross, roughly?

Advisor: Okay. Before we get too far into goals, I want to make sure the household basics are right—what country are we planning around for you two?
Client 1: US—yeah, we’re in the US.
Advisor: Great, thanks Jennifer. And just so I’m clear on how you want to work together—are you engaging me through an RIA, a registered investment advisor relationship, or something else?
Client 2: RIA (registered investment advisor).
Advisor: Perfect. And risk-wise—when markets get choppy, do you see yourselves as conservative, moderate, or more growth-oriented?

Advisor: Great, thanks Jennifer. And just so I’m clear on how you want to work together—are you engaging me through an RIA, a registered investment advisor relationship, or something else?
Client 2: RIA (registered investment advisor).
Advisor: Perfect. And risk-wise—when markets get choppy, do you see yourselves as conservative, moderate, or more growth-oriented?
Client 1: Growth. I mean—growth, but with a plan.
Advisor: Got it. When you say “with a plan,” is that more about keeping enough cash for near-term needs, or just not taking unnecessary bets?

Client 2: And I want a clean plan—like, what to do with the accounts and how tax-efficient we are.
Advisor: Perfect. Let’s start with the cash-flow basics, then we’ll map the accounts and any insurance. Jennifer, William—what do you have coming in in total each year, gross, roughly?
Client 2: Combined gross is 63600 a year.
Advisor: Got it—thank you. And on the spending side, what do your total monthly expenses usually run, all-in?
Client 1: We track it pretty closely—3050 a month.

Advisor: Okay—so goals are coming through as stability on retirement timing for you, Jennifer, and tax efficiency for you, William. Before we build the timeline, I want to make sure I’ve got each of your work situations right.
Advisor: Jennifer, starting with you—what’s your gross annual income right now, just ballpark is fine and then we’ll pin it down?
Client 1: It’s $44,200 gross annually.
Advisor: Got it. And in the household, you’re the primary person I should anchor the plan around—does that feel right?
Client 1: Yes—my household role is primary.

Advisor: Great. William, anything more specialized—private investments, private markets, that sort of thing?
Client 2: Yeah, I’ve got a private markets piece on a retirement platform—16700.
Advisor: Okay—thank you. Now, I also want to capture the home, even though it’s not investable in the same way. Jennifer, do you consider your primary residence part of the picture here—what’s the current value you use?
Client 1: Yes—primary residence value is 275300.
Advisor: Got it. And just to confirm on liabilities: besides the fact you said no monthly debt payments, do you have any loans outstanding at all right now?

Advisor: Got it. And in the household, you’re the primary person I should anchor the plan around—does that feel right?
Client 1: Yes—my household role is primary.
Advisor: Thanks. And how would you describe your occupation group—like professional, skilled trades, self-employed—what bucket fits best?
Client 1: Professional.
Advisor: Perfect. One admin thing—just so I label things correctly in my notes: Jennifer, you’re Client 1, correct?

Advisor: Hi Jennifer, hi William—nice to meet you both. I’m glad you came in. Before we get into the weeds, what prompted you to set this up now?
Client 1: Thanks. For me it’s retirement timing and just… making sure we’re not missing anything big.
Client 2: And I want a clean plan—like, what to do with the accounts and how tax-efficient we are.

Client 2: Correct, I’m Client 2—2.

Client 2: It’s around twenty-ish, but the exact gross annual income is 19450.
Advisor: Got it—thank you. And that 19450 is before taxes and deductions, just gross pay, right?
Client 2: Yes, gross.

Advisor: Are you currently employed, or are you doing consulting or anything like that?
Client 2: I’m employed.
Advisor: Okay, thanks. And in the household, I have you down as Jennifer’s spouse/partner—does that match how you’d describe your role here?
Client 2: Yes, spouse/partner.
Advisor: Great. One slightly nerdy admin thing—just so I don’t mix you two up in my notes: William, you’re Client 2, correct?

Client 1: Yes, I’m Client 1—1.

Client 2: Yes, that’s right—those are the two income lines, monthly, $17,250 annual on the salary and $46,400 annual on my bonus.
Advisor: Okay. And expenses were $3,050 per month—still the right baseline for planning?
